author: @makerslife
text:
This was so plain simple I did it right away! I used an HB#2 and an H8 pencil. Now what was interesting is when I went from drawing it on paper to directly on a quartz crystal. The increase in power out was tremendous. I went from getting 20-30 uV to 0.182 V with a similar size circuit!!!! I repeated the process multiple times with similar results. I'm not sure why this is, but seemed very interesting to me and thought you would like to hear about my results. Maybe you can give it a go and see if you have similar results.

Some interesting finds I had:
 - I found that the size of the crystal didn't seem to matter. 
- At 300°C it no longer produced a voltage
-Freezing prior to applying heat seemed to be more of a detriment
-Applying heat to the Cathode produced more voltage 

Thanks for all of your videos!

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Then comes the question: can you print out as much power as the cost of the ink and the electricity it took to make it?

The voltage gap would surely need to be pretty tall to recover the cost!

Perhaps the trick would be to stack it and make it a 3D printed thermopile.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to print a single unit would be parts of parts of a penny mate - I mean really really small - I worked on a machine that can product - and I am not kidding - 1 kilometre of paper in 1 minute - it boggles the mind how fast and how much some of these machines make - to get a grasp on it  - it might be good to think how much a newspaper costs and how many hundreds of thousands of these units could be printed in a newspaper

replies.author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Definitely the smaller each junction, the cheaper the cost, and the more one can pack into a small point. 

I've been working on how to build a huge printer of my own [I am an author, besides being a nerd] and it would be as good for printing these little electrical gates as it would be at printing books, only one might opt for wider and longer 'trim sizes' than a normal book, depending on their intended application.

Like say if this were something one wanted to put in the walls or roof of a house, one could print pieces that fit between the rafters and the studs and doubled as insulation at the same time...

Say pick an arbitrary width [most houses are built 16" on center here in the USA, no idea about any other countries] and print the material out either as a continuous 16" roll and add some kind of adapter clip wherever you needed to cut and splice it, OR, pre-print [since it would be digital and on-demand] fixed lengths for every spare space in a home, as it is being built, with ends already set up to just apply some kind of adhesive connection strip, and if you recall you mentioned using zinc chloride to make an extra tough, resilient type of paper, this would make it more durable, so you could stack it and even make 'batts' [like batts of fiberglass insulation] that could handle being rolled up from the factory, shipped on a pallet to the house and just pasted into the cavities between the studs, before people even went into wiring the home, and, in fact, it would become part of the house wiring, after an effect, so the same places one would run collection passages,they could also run their conduit for high voltage, and it would take up almost no additional space, and scarcely much more copper wire.

I could see setting up to just mass print these for years on end if they prove to be cost effective enough.

I am already setting up to experiment with mass producing zinc chloride treated paper based off your video, if my idea works it is only logical to follow up with the printed [would you call them 'diodes' or just 'semiconductors'?] in massive spools, using 100% recycled paper, therefore at the same time, keeping paper out of the waste stream and all the nastiness that goes with that kind of disposal.

[I have been collecting literally thousands of regular brown paper feed sacks from my own farm as well as a neighboring farm and I have a small truck load of them already. Each feed sack is roughly .8 of a square meter of pepper times 5 separate layers, so roughly 4 square meters of paper each]

Since people already recycle paper, and its pretty much useless for printing on at that point, but useful for other things, and I know where I can get literal truck lads of shredded document paper, it would be pretty easy to develop a system which takes any kind of scrap paper [white, brown, colored, newspaper or print type, really doesn't matter], make huge strips of this durable paper, and then print the collectors to them in a non-stop continuous project.

It would be serving the environment, AND he public on SO MANY levels too! (~_^)-b

author: @Xero1of1
text:
Hmm. Okay... my brain is trying to work here... 1 piece of paper, three printers with three different kinds of ink. The first two would be for the thermocouple. The third is copper-based. (I don't know if you need the third...) So it'd be like two trees growing close together... the copper makes up the trunk and transports the electricity generated. The branches of the two trees that interact are the first two inks. You design a very simple but very tightly repeating pattern with thousands of 'trees' on a single piece of paper. You break this into three different files, one of each printer/ink. Print the paper through the first, let it dry a bit. Then print the same paper through the second and again on the third. Then repeat this with more sheets until you have something like a 6'x3' area of paper. You then laminate each page to protect the ink and prevent any moisture from getting in there and messing things up. Then you get a 6'x3' thin copper sheet (or foil) and paint one side black. Treat the other side with something thermally conductive to prevent oxidation. Adhere the sheets once the electrical connections are made to the non-painted end of the copper sheet and build a black box around it with a glass top. Going for the greenhouse effect here, but on steroids. Then attach another, non-painted copper sheet to the bottom to make the cool side. Then just set it out in the sun. You could solder some fins to the bottom side to help with heat dissipation or manufacture some sort of cheap heat sink. New kind of solar panel that uses heat alone instead of photons to produce electricity. It should also last quite a bit longer than solar, I would think.

Could be an interesting project? You'd need some sort of strip of diodes for each of the 'trunks' on one side of the papers to control the electrical flow. The only particular part about the diodes is they'd have to have incredibly low Gate voltage/current so you're not wasting power on the diodes...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: maybe a fractal design? like a fractal antenna?

replies.author: @Xero1of1
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Fractals are interesting... but I think you'd need an extremely precise printer for that... With trees and everything being more-or-less straight up and down on the sheet, I think you could do more with more common, less-expensive printers...

replies.author: @travismoore7849
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering So far I made a small acetone lamp from an old transformer for copper wire. An old Hernandez jar cleaned up for the lamp. Holes in the lid for the bolts to hold my filaments and for air. Then using a blow torch to turn the copper into black copper oxide. All that is needed is a bit of acetone to have the catalyst reaction to make the filaments glow.

replies.author: @travismoore7849
replies.text: Acetone Lamp Mark 2: Take a lid and make a hole in the center. Put a bolt with threads in the top. With a small wooden wheel that is smaller than the lid hole. Take some copper screen and heat treat it. And put the small wood wheel on the bolt and fasten the mesh copper screen on the the wheel making sure it reaches the lid. Make sure two air holes are in the top of the lid in the diameter of the screen for vents. One or two more holes are made for vents but these have pipes running though them and down and up through two holes in the wood wheel. Then a piece of carbon felt is put in the bottom of the jar. Acetone up to an inch or so is poured in. Then the lid is taken off with the copper screen assembly preheated a bit. Then put back into the jar. The carbon felt only goes up two inches and the screen and bolt only go down two or three inches in the assembly. As acetone vapors react with oxygen it gets hot and glows. Convection draws more air into the device making it glow hotter.

author: @johnfitzgerald8656
text:
Hi Robert, 
just a brief note to say I love your videos, both thought provoking & funny. I'm convinced that if whatever you're doing doesn't make you smile you should stop doing it.
I saw your earlier comments about abusive bots causing aggravation online & couldn't agree more with you. They should sod off.
Ref wind experiments; I made a vibration device connected to a speaker & got some microvoltage but nothing really useful. I'm getting some stuff together for an improved version (hopefully) & I'll post details, if I get useful output.
Meanwhile, a whacky idea of yours caught my attention, the underpants wind turbine. I almost fell over laughing at that. We have a lot of tent fabric left over from another daft idea & I'm playing around with a larger version of your underpants turbine. I'll report more on this if I haven't been carried away by men in white coats.
Chin up & on we go.
John Fitzgerald

replies.text: it would if you were the hot side but it would cool the ambient if that's above body temp - so win win

author: @PsiQ
text:
I was expecting you to use the pencil complete.. was surprised it worked that well with drawings :-)

BMW developed a improved peltier version to win back electric power from exhaust heat.. never went into production because the output vs the cost/weight (even optimized) was so bad, it did not matter for fuel saving achieved with it...
You get a lot more usable easy energy when using the alternator for soft braking when backing off the digital throttle or rolling downhill.

Problem with the peltiers is (only 17% efficiency if at optimal point) that you need a high temp diff side to side, but the modules transfer heat kinda good. So at a certain input (or output) = temp difference you get a thermal "short" through the module.
Now if you stack two modules they have double the heat flow resistance but also only see half the temp difference.. and so on.

I did a presentation on those things in school physics 6th grade something over 2 decades ago.

Cool thing is you can create ice out of thin (humid) air and its a special feeling to touch something that is both hot and cold at the same time, especially if you explain it only afterwards, maybe bet something.. ;-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: me too - it was a really interesting find in so many ways

author: @jb-dn3ct
text: This subject has always fascinated me, if I remember right feeding current into them will make one side get hot and the other cold,  if it was able to be more efficient it would definitely be a very useful device, another subject that I think about alot is how trees move water up so far, Robert did do a video that touched on this a bit which was really nice to see

replies.text: it is indeed a fascinating subject - the thing on trees too!

replies.author: @jb-dn3ct
replies.text: @ianbutler1983 that's good to know, seems they have a fairly short lifespan when used in this way then, my dad has one that runs a fan on his wood burner, has lasted about 5 years so far, I've started making a methanol heater after being inspired from watching Robert's video, wanting to make it a bit more sturdy but maintaining as little cost as possible I've used an old gas bottle (filled with water and left for a week as I don't fancy an explosion) and a scrap stainless steel bin, even has easy fitting for a flue at the top, I will be using one of these thermo couples, I'll need to fabricate a flat base at the top for it to sit on but I'm hoping it's going to make a nice finishing touch

replies.author: @jb-dn3ct
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I've been meaning to test a theory for some time now, I have half built a meter long vacuum chamber from scrap toughened glass, the idea was to run thin tubes through the middle painted black to absorb infrared, the tubes to sit in a container of water at the bottom to be kept cold, the vacuum chamber in direct sunlight and then the tubes exit out the top into a collector, the idea was to see if it was possible to collect enough heat from the sun into the water to overcome the tension at the end of the tubes, after watching your video on the evaporative cooling effect on water tension I'm now looking at trying to incorporate this at the top

author: @mikejones-vd3fg
text:
Salvaged 2 of these(peltier devices) from a humidifier i found the back in the summer, tried to see if i could use the heat from my solar panel to create electricity usnig these, if you put heat on them they produce a voltage so a good way to perhaps capture excess heat.  I couldnt get more then 50mv so it wasnt that good, and going the otehr way, trying to cool the panels with this by applying electricty, actaully worked but took too much energy , at best i think it evened out, the price of cooling was the amount of energy i got due to cooling  panels, maybe if you had the whole panel covered it might increase exponentially towarad a net gain and could be worth cooling as panels can loose upto 30%  efficiencey when overheated.... but  i didnt get that far.  
I did  try to simualte an AC voltage as youve probably noticed rubbing one side creates a positive voltage, rubbing the other side create a negative, so in theory if your rub it in the right timing you should be able to create an AC voltage?  easier said then done, i couldnt get the timing right.  Then I jsut put the thing between my laptop and the cooler it was sitting on (targus laptop cooler/stand), and voila!  2.35v  AC from 2 of these in parallel just from the heat from my laptop on one side, and being cooled by a fan on the other.    Im not sure if the laptop was blowing or sucking air to be honest so i dont know how reproducable this is - a stable ac voltage, maybe i just had the right fan speeds  but WOW 2.35v AC vs 50mv DC .  Thing is i cant really think of a place to expoit a hot an cold side to make this really effetive, perhaps floating on water, where the one side is cooled by water and the other side is heated by the sun. Or better yet, space! there you'd have 300c on the sun facing side and -300 on your back for the cold side.  Perfect place for these devices, probably could provide massive energy and perhaps the key to interstellar travel.  Anyway ive sort of rambled about this but wanted to share, i was able to get AC voltage from a peltier device and i havent  heard of this being done anywhere so maybe this info can help somone in their pursuites of free enregy.   Theres one last option, make energy! why not? its been done before, maybe it can happen again? but according to science energy cant be made or destroyed... drats! but wait, its here, so there was a mechanism that made it happen, so why not again?

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
Another good one, Rob!

There's a lot of demonstrations out there (not just this one) that show they can light a light bulb or spin a fan, but I'm wondering, now that we've got them, if it's feasible to build up to "can it charge a cel phone or a cellphone charger?"  Would seem to be a lot easier than charging a car battery, as was about the only alternative when the "light bulb" test became popular.  

Not sure about the lithium packs though. How would they hold up under uneven charging? Would it degrade them faster? Would they burst into flames?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.text:
You could very easily create thousands of contact points in a single shot.  It would allow you to use any conductive ink/material you wanted.


I have been thinking of making a attachment for my woodstove chimney. Print the contacts on a very thin layer of fiber wool. In a circular pattern. That should provide a huge gradient in temperature fairly easily. If you laminated them and sleeved a stove pipe into the pile.

Creating contacts to the outside diameter would probably be tricky.

text: We aren't to use our children as labourers? Since when!? Child labo(u)r was a foundational principle when I was coming up.

replies.author: @ricksanchez3176
replies.text:
We have a lot of Amish locally. Talk to an Amish kid, they look right at you, know what they are talking about, and enjoy playing ball and family. Average "Anglish" kid, spaced out look, and don't really enjoy actual fun, because leisure is all they know, and don't appreciate family, because family is just a source for material things.  Don't get me wrong, exploitation, disposable labor force, no, but enough work to develope a sense of worth and purpose, heck yeah.

I should note, "English" is what they call us Yanks, lol.
